text: It is safer to be feared than loved if you cannot be both. Love is held by a chain of obligation that men, being selfish, break the moment it suits them; fear is sustained by dread of punishment, which never abandons you.
A prince should be both the lion and the fox — strong enough to terrify wolves and cunning enough to recognise traps. Whoever relies on the lion alone does not understand power.

Cruelties are well used when they are inflicted at one stroke from necessity for self-protection, then discontinued. Cruelties badly used are those which start few but grow with time — these breed perpetual resentment.
Mercenary and auxiliary armies are useless and dangerous: a prince who rests his state on them stands on sand. Found your power on your own arms or you found it on nothing.
A prince must learn how not to be good — and to use this knowledge or refrain, according to necessity. Trying to live by virtue in a world that does not will be his ruin.
Princes who attempt to be too generous end up taxing the people to fund their reputation. Better to acquire the name of miser early — it preserves the resources, the people, and the state.
The prince does not need to actually possess all the virtues — religious, faithful, humane, sincere — but he must seem to. Most men judge by what they see; few touch what you really are.

What men chiefly resent is the loss of their patrimony. A prince must above all keep his hands off his subjects' property and women — for men forget the death of their fathers more quickly than the loss of their inheritance.
Conquered states accustomed to their own laws should either be destroyed, occupied in person, or governed through a friendly oligarchy that owes its position to you. Half-measures invite revolt.
Fortune is a river that can be channelled by foresight. Half of what we do is hers; the other half — or close to it — she leaves for us to govern. The bold and adaptable command her best.
Whoever is the cause of another becoming powerful is ruined: he provides resources or reputation, both of which the new power will use against him.
A prince should never permit himself to be despised — through frivolity, effeminacy, indecision, or weakness. Hatred can be survived; contempt destroys.
